items.q: Is the speaker of a poem always the poet?
items.correct: No - the speaker is a persona who may be quite different from the poet.
items.misconception: The 'I' in a poem is always the poet themselves.
items.why: Treating the speaker as a constructed voice avoids wrongly reading every poem as autobiography.

items.correct: When a sentence runs over the line break without a pause.
items.misconception: When two lines rhyme with each other.
items.random_wrong: When a poem has no punctuation at all.
items.why: Enjambment is about syntax flowing past the line ending, controlling pace - not about rhyme.

items.q: How should you tackle an unseen poem in an exam?
items.correct: Read closely for meaning, methods and effects, then build an argument.
items.misconception: Guess the poet and write out their biography.
items.random_wrong: Just summarise the plot of the poem.
items.why: Unseen response rewards analysis of how the poem works, not background or paraphrase.

items.q: When analysing an unseen poem, you should first focus on:
items.correct: The poem's meaning, methods and effects
items.why: Correct answer: The poem's meaning, methods and effects. “The poet's biography” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: A sentence runs over a line break without a pause
items.misconception: A line ends with a full stop
items.why: Correct answer: A sentence runs over a line break without a pause. “A line ends with a full stop” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The 'speaker' or persona of a poem:
items.correct: May be a voice distinct from the poet
items.why: The answer examiners want is: May be a voice distinct from the poet. “Is always the poet” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A volta in a poem is a:
items.correct: Turn or shift in argument or mood
items.why: Correct answer: Turn or shift in argument or mood. “Type of rhyme” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
